title: AI Trust & Safety Digest
tagline: A curated current snapshot of AI trust, safety, and governance, including as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
lead: Today’s signal is concentrated in pre-deployment scrutiny, evaluation design, and emerging agent-security research. The strongest updates come from an external assessment of a frontier model, plus new papers probing interpretability, reward hacking, and attack detection in agentic systems—together pointing to a field still improving how it measures and hardens advanced models before and during deployment.

items.title: METR publishes predeployment evaluation of GPT-5.6 Sol
items.why_it_matters: Independent predeployment evidence can shape deployment decisions, safeguards, and risk communication for frontier models.

items.title: OpenAI previews GPT-5.6 Sol with updated safety stack
items.why_it_matters: A frontier-model preview matters when paired with explicit safety-stack claims and external evaluation context.

items.title: Human Creativity Benchmark argues evaluator disagreement is signal, not noise
items.why_it_matters: It challenges simplistic evaluation assumptions that can mismeasure model behavior in subjective domains.

items.title: Forensic Trajectory Signatures proposes detection method for agent memory poisoning
items.why_it_matters: It offers a concrete behavioral approach to spotting persistent memory-poisoning attacks in LLM agents.

items.title: MESA prioritizes vulnerable communication channels in multi-agent systems
items.why_it_matters: It targets a growing security problem: how to allocate defenses across agent-to-agent attack surfaces.

items.title: C²R aims to improve sparse autoencoder reliability for interpretability
items.why_it_matters: Better SAE feature quality could make mechanistic interpretability results more stable and trustworthy.

items.title: Pessimism's Paradox finds conservative offline training can worsen reward hacking after online adaptation
items.why_it_matters: It questions a common safety intuition about offline-to-online training pipelines for reasoning models.

items.title: Partnership on AI highlights AI bias risks for LGBTQIA+ people
items.why_it_matters: Bias affecting protected groups remains a core deployment risk with governance and accountability implications.

note: Selected from recent trust-and-safety relevant posts and papers by labs, evaluators, standards bodies, policy organizations, and arXiv, then summarized with GPT-5.4. Includes as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
